narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 42% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 and LSAT score of 39, alongside your Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles within your school, your lower-middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. The most influential factor pushing the model toward predicting bar failure was your Year 3 decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year—a stark contrast to the top-half averages seen among students who typically pass the bar. This low ranking carried the strongest weight in the decision, signaling to the model a potential struggle to build momentum in later undergraduate years. On a more positive note, your LSAT score stood out as the second-most important factor, pulling strongly in favor of predicting success since it exceeds the average of 37.27 among those who pass the bar. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA, while below the 3.24 average for bar passers, still contributed positively by nudging the prediction toward passing rather than failing. These top three elements—your Year 3 performance weighing heaviest against you, tempered by your stronger LSAT and solid overall GPA—ultimately tipped the scales toward rejection, even as factors like your full-time status aligned with the 93% of successful students. Compared to admitted applicants, who generally show higher decile rankings in later years and slightly stronger GPAs, your profile appeared riskier for bar passage despite some standout strengths. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your LSAT achievement highlighting real potential. To move forward, consider retaking courses or pursuing targeted prep to boost your later-year deciles, perhaps through a post-bac program or additional undergrad work. Strengthening your GPA closer to or above 3.24 could also help in future applications. Many students in similar situations reapply successfully after addressing these gaps—reach out to admissions for personalized advice on bolstering your record.
